title: Price tumbles spread to all capital cities
full_text:
Tumbling house prices are spreading to capital cities outside Sydney and Melbourne, one of the world’s biggest banks has declared, warning Labor that the first home buyers it wants to help with its property tax changes are unlikely to want to “catch the falling knife” of devalued homes.
HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham issued the stark forecast on Monday that falling home values in the month after Jim Chalmers’ May budget were “just the beginning” of a downward path for property prices across the country.
The bank’s central case forecasts national prices to tumble by up to 8 per cent to the end of 2027, as Labor’s changes to negative gearing and capital gains tax – as well as the Reserve Bank’s three rate hikes – sap investor demand from the market.
Contrary to claims from Anthony Albanese and the Treasurer that young Australians are entering the market without any contest from investors, Mr Bloxham said it was unlikely that first home buyers and owner-occupiers would risk entering the market during such a severe downturn.
“As we see it, first home buyers and other owner-occupiers are unlikely to want to try to ‘catch a falling knife’,” he said.
“With no rate cuts expected anytime soon we expect no near-term circuit breaker, which means the housing price correction is likely to continue for some time yet … Hold on to your hats.” Cotality’s home value index dropped 0.4 per cent nationally in June, led by severe falls in Sydney and Melbourne of 3.2 per cent and 2.6 per cent respectively.
One of the country’s largest mortgage brokers last week flagged the number of prospective home buyers applying for loans had plummeted by 20 per cent since Dr Chalmers handed down his controversial tax changes.
“Our central case is for housing prices to decline in H2 (the second half of) 2026 and fall by 2-6 per cent over 2027,” Mr Bloxham said.
“The pace of decline in the June figures suggests downside risk to our central forecast. Rate cuts are likely to be needed to deliver a turnaround, but we see these as likely to be a year away.” After the RBA handed down three rate hikes in the first three meetings this year in its fight against inflation, the average retail lending rate now sits at about 6 per cent, likely warding off highly leveraged first home buyers using the governments 5 per cent deposit scheme.
All but a handful of economists see rates remaining elevated well into next year, with Westpac’s chief economist Luci Ellis tipping another rate rise before the central bank considers cutting in early 2028.
“If house prices are falling, the assessment that any buyer is going to be trying to make is: when are they going to reach their bottom and when are they going start rising,” Mr Bloxham said. “Buying into a falling market is tricky because house prices might fall further yet.
“To be sure, there is more uncertainty than usual about the housing price forecasts, given the tax system changes. Negative gearing has been a feature of the tax system for decades and the capital gains tax discount has been in place since 1999.” “Removing these all at once has no recent historical precedent to help draw firm conclusions and may make this housing correction quite different to previous, often interest-rate driven, housing price cycles.” Auction clearance rates have been below 50 per cent for the past three weeks, according to figures from property data firm Cotality.
“The combined capitals’ preliminary clearance rate found a floor over the past three weeks, holding in the high 40 per cent range,” said Cotality research director Tim Lawless.
Low clearance rates have usually been a leading indicator of falling prices. The Australian revealed last week that some of the most popular suburbs for the federal first-home buyer guarantee had seen property values fall, with Australia’s newest homeowners staring down the prospect of negative equity.
KPMG director of planning economics Terry Rawnsley said he expected a three to six-month period of price falls during which prospective first-home buyers would sit on the sidelines until prices jumped back.
“First home buyers are pretty conservative, they wouldn’t want to try and time the bottom of the market precisely so they don’t want to jump in too early,” Mr Rawnsley said.
Westpac senior economist Pat Bustamante said prospective first home buyers would “probably” be put off by high interest rates and uncertain economic conditions.
“Prices are coming down and making home ownership more affordable,” Mr Bustamante said.
“What we’ve seen in the past is first-home buyers … they respond to what interest rates are doing and how confident they are in the economy.
“The market is, at the moment, sitting on potentially another hike – that has been fully priced in.” He added that Westpac expected another two hikes. MORE REPORTS P4
